reasoning: Confirmed SSRF. The call chain is: authenticated admin uploads attacker-crafted .dat file -> _import() deserializes $data['mods'] from the file -> if 'cei-import-images' request param is set, _import_images($data['mods']) is called -> for each mod value matching _is_image_url() (preg_match for .jpg|.jpeg|.png|.gif in the string), _sideload_image($val) is called -> download_url($file) at line 418 is called with the attacker-controlled URL directly, with no scheme restriction and no host allowlist/blocklist. The _is_image_url() check at line 372 is bypass-trivial: any URL with an image extension anywhere in the path passes (e.g. http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/index.jpg). No call to wp_http_validate_url(), filter_var with FILTER_FLAG_NO_PRIV_RANGE, or any other host/scheme validation exists anywhere in the plugin. WordPress's download_url() uses wp_safe_remote_get() which blocks loopback (127.0.0.1) in some WP versions but does NOT block the IMDS endpoint 169.254.169.254 or other internal RFC-1918 hosts, making cloud metadata exfiltration viable. Auth requirement is correctly stated: can_import() requires current_user_can('install_plugins') (Administrator by default), plus a valid nonce. The severity is high because the attacker is server-side (cloud metadata, internal services), but slightly constrained by the admin-only auth gate.
